intro_text:
Kitsilano Beach faces English Bay from the north edge of the Kitsilano neighbourhood, across the water from downtown Vancouver and the North Shore mountains. Its Squamish name is X̱epx̱páy̓em, "having red cedar." The flats just east of the sand, where the Burrard Street Bridge now lands at the mouth of False Creek, were Sen̓áḵw, a Squamish village and the core of Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6, about 80 acres set aside in 1877. The district's English name is an anglicization of X̱ats'alanexw, the name of Squamish chief August Jack Khatsahlano (1877–1971); the Canadian Pacific Railway took the spelling "Kitsilano" around 1905 to market the land it was subdividing.

Before the Squamish were removed, a settler was too. In 1882 Sam Greer pre-empted 160 acres of the waterfront and farmed it, and the sand became known as Greer's Beach. When the province granted the land to the CPR, Greer refused to leave. In September 1891 he fired on a deputy sheriff who came to evict him; he was convicted of assault causing bodily harm that November and sentenced to 27 months of hard labour, and his house was pulled down. The beach kept his name into the early 1900s.

The Squamish clearance was larger and more lasting. In 1913 the provincial government, acting through its attorney general, arranged a surrender of the reserve that federal law did not permit, paying roughly twenty Squamish men about $11,250 each. Residents were given a few days to pack, loaded onto a barge, and taken to reserves on the North Shore and Howe Sound; the emptied houses were burned. By 1916 the Vancouver Harbour Commission had expropriated more of the flats, and by 1946 the Kitsilano reserve had been erased entirely.

The Squamish never accepted the loss. The nation sued Canada in 1977 over the mishandling of the reserve, voted in 2000 to accept a $92.5-million federal settlement, and had 11.7 acres (4.7 hectares) of the original reserve restored, confirmed through the courts in 2002 and marked by a homecoming of 28 canoes to the shore. In 2019 Squamish members voted to develop that land as Sen̓áḵw: eleven towers and roughly 6,000 rental homes at the south foot of the Burrard Bridge, described by the nation as the largest Indigenous-led housing project in Canadian history. Construction began in September 2022, backed by a $1.4-billion federal loan.

The beach in front of all this is Vancouver's most-used swimming shore. At its east end, Kitsilano Pool is a 137-metre saltwater outdoor pool, opened in 1931 and heated since a 1979 rebuild, the longest outdoor pool in North America, filled each season with English Bay seawater. Volleyball courts line the sand, the free Kitsilano Showboat stage has run summer performances since 1935, and the water averages under half a metre of wave height. The bay is not always clean: Vancouver Coastal Health posts recurring summer advisories when stormwater runoff pushes E. coli past safe limits.
spike_explainer:
Most Vancouver beaches are named for a landform or a settler. Kitsilano is named for a person the city pushed aside. The word is an anglicization of X̱ats'alanexw, the name of the Squamish chief August Jack Khatsahlano, and the beach and the flats behind it were Sen̓áḵw, a Squamish village and the heart of a reserve. The singular fact of this shoreline is not the mountain view or the heated pool. It is that the beach is the recreation face of Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6, a reserve that was not abandoned but taken, and that the people who were removed are, a century later, rebuilding on the land in plain sight of the sand.

**The reserve.** In 1877 the Joint Indian Reserve Commission set aside about 80 acres at the mouth of False Creek as Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6, formalising a Squamish village the Squamish call Sen̓áḵw. It was home to roughly twenty families. The site sat on some of the most valuable undeveloped waterfront in a city that was about to explode in size, directly across the water from the downtown peninsula and beside the rail and bridge lines the Canadian Pacific Railway wanted to run.

**Greer's Beach.** The Squamish were not the only people cleared from this shore. In 1882 the settler Sam Greer pre-empted 160 acres of the beachfront and farmed it, and for two decades the sand was Greer's Beach. When the province handed the land to the CPR, Greer fought back; in September 1891 he shot a deputy sheriff sent to evict him, was convicted of assault causing bodily harm, and served 27 months of hard labour while his house was demolished. His stand is remembered almost fondly in local lore. The far larger removal that followed is remembered much less.

**1913: the clearing.** In 1913 the provincial government, through its attorney general, arranged a surrender of the reserve that federal law explicitly did not allow, because the Indian Act barred the private sale of reserve land without proper federal authorisation. Eighteen Squamish men were paid about $11,250 each, some $202,500 in total for land now worth billions. The residents were given a few days, loaded onto a barge, and taken to reserves on the North Shore and in Howe Sound. The houses they left were burned. Newspapers of the day called it "Indians' Moving Day." The Vancouver Harbour Commission expropriated more of the site in 1916, and by 1946 the reserve had been wiped from the map.

**The name stayed; the people did not.** The bitter detail is in the branding. Around 1905 the CPR, subdividing the uplands it had been granted, adopted "Kitsilano" as the name of its new district, an anglicised version of Chief Khatsahlano's own name. The railway kept the chief's name to sell the lots and helped remove the people who bore it. For decades the beach was simply a popular swimming spot, its origin unmarked, while August Jack Khatsahlano lived on as one of the last direct witnesses to the pre-city Squamish world, his recollections recorded by Vancouver's first archivist and published as Conversations with Khahtsahlano.

**The return.** The Squamish never let the claim die. The nation sued Canada in 1977 over the mishandling of the reserve, and in 2000 its members voted to accept a $92.5-million federal settlement that, confirmed through the courts in 2002, restored 11.7 acres of the original reserve. The nation marked the homecoming by paddling 28 canoes back to the shore. What had been an erased reserve was legally a reserve again, a small wedge of land at the foot of the Burrard Bridge, and it was now some of the most valuable real estate in Canada.

**Sen̓áḵw now.** In 2019 Squamish members voted to build on it. The result, under construction since September 2022, is Sen̓áḵw: eleven towers and roughly 6,000 rental homes on 11.7 acres, financed in part by a $1.4-billion federal loan and described by the nation as the largest Indigenous-led housing and retail development in Canadian history. Because the land is a federal reserve, it sits outside City of Vancouver zoning, which is precisely why it can be built so much denser than the low-rise neighbourhood around it. The founding developer, Westbank, sold its stake to the pension manager OPTrust in 2025. The towers rise at the east end of the beach, visible from the sand.

**What the beach is.** The recreation lives on top of all this. Kitsilano Pool, opened in 1931 and heated since 1979, runs 137 metres along the east end and is the longest outdoor pool in North America, filled each season with English Bay seawater. Volleyball courts fill the sand in summer, the free Showboat stage has performed beside the pool since 1935, and the water is calm enough to swim most of the year, when Vancouver Coastal Health has not closed it for E. coli. But the reason this beach earns a page is not its amenities. It is that you can stand on Vancouver's busiest swimming shore and watch a nation rebuild the village the city burned, on the same ground, at the same time.
honest_reckoning_note:
The comfortable version of Kitsilano Beach is a swimming beach with a heated pool and a mountain view. The fuller version is that all of it sits on Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6, the Squamish village of Sen̓áḵw, and that the reserve did not fade away, it was taken. In 1913 the provincial government engineered a surrender that federal law forbade, paid eighteen men roughly $11,250 each, barged the residents to other reserves, and burned the houses they left. The name Vancouver kept for the district is an anglicization of a Squamish chief's name, applied by the railway that profited from clearing the land.

What makes Kitsilano unusual is that the reckoning is not only historical. The Squamish won part of the land back through the courts, and the Sen̓áḵw towers now rising at the east end of the beach are among the most visible corrections of a dispossession on the Vancouver waterfront, roughly 6,000 homes on 11.7 acres the nation had to sue for decades to recover. The beach is genuinely public and genuinely well used. It is also a place where the history is not settled background but a construction site you can watch from the sand.
reckoning_pullquote: The reserve did not fade away; it was taken, and the towers at the beach's east end are the Squamish taking part of it back.

key_facts.label: Reserve
key_facts.value: Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6, ~80 acres, set aside 1877

key_facts.label: Sen̓áḵw development
key_facts.value: 11 towers, ~6,000 rental homes; $1.4B federal loan; began 2022

key_facts.label: Kitsilano Pool
key_facts.value: 137 m saltwater, opened 1931, heated since 1979; longest outdoor pool in North America

key_facts.label: Name origin
key_facts.value: Anglicization of Chief X̱ats'alanexw (August Jack Khatsahlano), taken by the CPR c.1905

key_facts.label: Waves
key_facts.value: ~0.38 m annual mean; calm, generally swimmable

margin_notes.text: The CPR did not just echo the shape of Chief Khatsahlano's name; it took the name itself to sell subdivided lots while the people who bore it were pushed off the waterfront. August Jack Khatsahlano (1877–1971) became one of the last direct sources on the pre-Vancouver Squamish world.

Greer's stand made him a folk hero: on his release from prison the city held a civic reception in his honour. His fight and the Squamish eviction rhyme, a settler and a nation both cleared off the same shore by the same railway land grant, but only Greer's name lingered on the beach.

About $202,500 changed hands in 1913 for land now worth billions. The surrender breached the Indian Act's bar on selling reserve land without proper federal authorisation, which is why the courts eventually reopened it. Newspapers called the removal "Indians' Moving Day."

Because Sen̓áḵw sits on federal reserve land, it is exempt from City of Vancouver zoning and can build far denser than the surrounding low-rise blocks, the legal fact that makes 6,000 homes possible on 11.7 acres. Westbank was the founding partner; in 2025 it sold its stake to the pension manager OPTrust.

Kitsilano Pool is near the end of its service life. A January 2022 storm and king tide damaged it, and after about $3.5 million in repairs it reopened only in August 2024, then again late in June 2025. The city has begun studying a full replacement.

A swimming advisory is triggered when repeat samples exceed 400 E. coli per 100 mL. Leaking septic systems, boat discharge, and post-rain storm runoff are the usual causes; the advisories are seasonal and lift once counts fall.

day_in_time.dawn: First light comes over the mountains behind the city rather than off the water, since the beach faces roughly north across English Bay. The sand is quiet except for early swimmers doing lengths at the pool and walkers on the seawall path; the tide decides how much beach there is to have.
day_in_time.midday: By late morning in summer the volleyball courts are full and the pool is at capacity, the downtown skyline and freighters at anchor forming the backdrop. Shade is scarce on the open sand, and the bay water stays cool, generally in the mid-teens Celsius even at the height of summer.
day_in_time.golden: The long west-coast evenings are the beach's set piece: the sun drops toward the North Shore and the Salish Sea, the towers of Sen̓áḵw catch the last light at the east end, and the Showboat stage runs its free program beside the pool.
day_in_time.night: After dark the sand empties while the seawall keeps its walkers and cyclists. The pool closes, the bay goes black beyond the lit path, and the cranes over the Sen̓áḵw site stand floodlit at the foot of the Burrard Bridge.

food_drink.name: The beachfront concession
food_drink.description: The two-storey pavilion on the sand housed the Watermark restaurant (2005–2010) and then The Boathouse, which closed on 21 September 2025; the Hawksworth Restaurant Group won the lease but has not yet opened a new concept there. The licensed poolside concession by Kitsilano Pool runs seasonally as Batch Kitsilano, serving DownLow Chicken fried chicken, beer, and canned cocktails from a patio on the beach.
food_drink.where: Kitsilano Beach Park, 2305 Cornwall Avenue, by the pool

food_drink.name: BC spot prawns
food_drink.description: A short wild fishery off the British Columbia coast, in season for roughly six weeks from May, and marked in Vancouver by an annual spot-prawn festival. They are a restaurant and market item, not sold on the beach.
food_drink.where: Vancouver restaurants and the Granville Island market, in late spring

food_drink.name: Wild Pacific salmon
food_drink.description: Sockeye, chinook, and coho from the coastal fishery are the regional signature, grilled or cured; the salmon runs are central to Squamish and wider Coast Salish food culture.

food_drink.name: Nanaimo bar
food_drink.description: A no-bake three-layer dessert of a crumb base, custard middle, and chocolate top, named for the Vancouver Island city of Nanaimo and a British Columbia staple in bakeries and cafes.
food_drink.where: Kitsilano cafes and bakeries along West 4th Avenue

timeline.title: Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6 set aside
timeline.description: The Joint Indian Reserve Commission formalises about 80 acres at the mouth of False Creek as Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6, covering the Squamish village of Sen̓áḵw, then home to roughly twenty families.

timeline.title: Sam Greer pre-empts the waterfront
timeline.description: The settler Sam Greer pre-empts 160 acres of the beachfront and farms it, and the sand becomes widely known as Greer's Beach, a name it keeps into the early 1900s.

timeline.title: Greer shoots a deputy sheriff during his eviction
timeline.description: With the province granting his land to the CPR, Sam Greer fires on a deputy sheriff sent to evict him. He is convicted of assault causing bodily harm on 16 November 1891 and sentenced to 27 months of hard labour; his house is demolished.

timeline.title: The Squamish are evicted and Sen̓áḵw burned
timeline.description: The provincial government arranges an unlawful surrender of the reserve, paying eighteen Squamish men roughly $11,250 each. Residents are given a few days, barged to reserves on the North Shore and Howe Sound, and their emptied homes are burned.

timeline.title: Kitsilano Pool opens
timeline.description: The 137-metre saltwater outdoor pool opens on 15 August 1931, built as a Depression-era job-creation project alongside other Vancouver saltwater pools, of which it is now the only one left.

timeline.title: Kitsilano Showboat begins
timeline.description: A free open-air performance stage opens beside the pool at the beach's east end. The Showboat has run summer programs of music and dance since 1935 and remains free to the public.

timeline.title: Squamish accept a $92.5-million settlement
timeline.description: Following a 1977 lawsuit over the reserve's mishandling, Squamish members vote to accept a $92.5-million federal settlement that, confirmed through the courts in 2002, restores 11.7 acres of the original reserve. The nation paddles 28 canoes back to the shore.

timeline.title: Construction begins on Sen̓áḵw
timeline.description: Building starts on Sen̓áḵw, eleven towers and roughly 6,000 rental homes on the restored reserve at the foot of the Burrard Bridge, backed by a $1.4-billion federal loan and billed by the nation as the largest Indigenous-led development in Canadian history.

timeline.title: Kitsilano Pool reopens after storm damage
timeline.description: After a January 2022 storm and king tide damaged the seaside pool, some $3.5 million in repairs allow it to reopen on 7 August 2024. It reopens late again in June 2025, and the city begins planning a full replacement.

zones.name: The main crescent
zones.character: The central sweep of sand where most swimmers and sunbathers gather, facing north across English Bay to downtown and the North Shore mountains. The substrate is feldspar-rich quartz sand and the water is calm most of the year.
zones.best_for: Swimming, sunbathing, and the west-coast sunset
zones.notes: Bay water stays cool (mid-teens Celsius) even in summer; watch for posted E. coli advisories after heavy rain.

zones.name: Kitsilano Pool and Showboat
zones.character: The east end, anchored by the 137-metre heated saltwater Kitsilano Pool, the longest outdoor pool in North America, with the free Showboat performance stage beside it.
zones.best_for: Lap swimming, families, and free summer performances
zones.notes: The outdoor pool runs roughly late May to September; recent storm damage delayed its 2024 and 2025 openings.

zones.name: Beach volleyball courts
zones.character: The banks of beach volleyball courts on the sand and grass behind the central beach, one of the busiest organised volleyball scenes in the city through the summer.
zones.notes: Courts fill on summer weekends; competitive play dominates peak hours.

zones.name: The east end and Sen̓áḵw
zones.character: The quieter eastern edge toward Vanier Park and the Burrard Bridge, where the restored reserve and the rising Sen̓áḵw towers meet the beach. This is the ground of Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6.
zones.best_for: Reading the beach's history from the sand; walking to Vanier Park
zones.notes: The Sen̓áḵw construction site is active and fenced; the towers are visible along the shore.

landmarks.name: Kitsilano Pool
landmarks.description: The 137-metre saltwater outdoor pool at the beach's east end, opened in 1931, heated since a 1979 rebuild, and the longest outdoor pool in North America. It is filled each season with seawater drawn from English Bay.

landmarks.name: Sen̓áḵw
landmarks.description: The Squamish Nation's development on the restored 11.7-acre reserve at the foot of the Burrard Bridge: eleven towers and roughly 6,000 rental homes, under construction since 2022 and billed as the largest Indigenous-led development in Canadian history.

landmarks.name: Burrard Street Bridge
landmarks.description: The 1932 art-deco bridge that lands at the beach's east end, crossing False Creek directly over the old reserve flats where the Sen̓áḵw towers now rise.

cultural_refs.title: August Jack Khatsahlano
cultural_refs.description: The Squamish chief (1877–1971) whose name, X̱ats'alanexw, the CPR anglicised into "Kitsilano." He was one of the last direct witnesses to the pre-city Squamish world.

cultural_refs.title: Conversations with Khahtsahlano
cultural_refs.creator: J.S. Matthews
cultural_refs.description: Vancouver's first city archivist, Major J.S. Matthews, recorded years of conversations with Khatsahlano and published them, preserving Squamish accounts of the land before the city.

cultural_refs.title: Khatsahlano Street Party
cultural_refs.creator: Kitsilano West 4th Avenue Business Association
cultural_refs.description: A ten-block free music and arts festival on West 4th Avenue, run since 2011 and named to acknowledge the chief whose name the neighbourhood carries.

cultural_refs.title: Sen̓áḵw
cultural_refs.description: The Squamish village name for the flats at the mouth of False Creek, now the name of the nation's development on the restored reserve beside the beach.

recurring_events.name: Kitsilano Showboat
recurring_events.description: Free open-air music and dance performances on the Showboat stage beside Kitsilano Pool, run by volunteers since 1935.

recurring_events.name: Khatsahlano Street Party
recurring_events.description: Ten blocks of West 4th Avenue, between Burrard and Macdonald streets, close for around fifty free concerts, food, and art, run since 2011 by the neighbourhood business association.

businesses.name: Batch Kitsilano Beach
businesses.description: The licensed seasonal concession and patio at the Kitsilano Pool building on the sand, run by the Vancouver bar brand Batch in partnership with the Park Board, serving DownLow Chicken fried chicken, craft beer, and canned cocktails. It replaces the older beach concession fare and is one of the few places to eat directly on the beach while the former Boathouse pavilion sits between operators.
businesses.address: Kitsilano Beach Park, 2305 Cornwall Avenue, Vancouver

things_to_know.label: The water is cold, and sometimes closed
things_to_know.note: English Bay stays in the mid-teens Celsius even at the height of summer, and Vancouver Coastal Health posts E. coli swimming advisories after heavy rain. Check current beach water-quality notices before swimming.

things_to_know.label: The pool's season is short and uncertain
things_to_know.note: Kitsilano Pool is outdoor and runs roughly late May to September, but storm damage delayed both its 2024 and 2025 openings. Confirm with the Vancouver Park Board before relying on it.

things_to_know.label: You are standing on a reserve
things_to_know.note: The beach and the flats east to the Burrard Bridge were Kitsilano Indian Reserve No. 6, the Squamish village of Sen̓áḵw. The towers at the east end are the nation's return to the land, not scenery.

things_to_know.label: Arrive early on summer weekends
things_to_know.note: Free street parking around Cornwall Avenue and Arbutus Street fills by late morning on sunny summer days; TransLink buses from downtown are the easier approach.

access_note: Kitsilano Beach is about 9.3 km by road from Vancouver International Airport (YVR) and roughly 3 km from downtown across the Burrard or Granville bridges. TransLink buses reach the neighbourhood from downtown and the Canada Line; free street parking around Cornwall Avenue and Arbutus Street fills early on summer weekends.
